title: CalTrust: Adaptive Trust Calibration via Contextual Bandits
keyTakeaways: We built a LinUCB contextual bandit that selects among four calibration interventions (none, calibrate only, comparison, reflection) based on user context and output characteristics.
An XGBoost trust predictor (AUC 0.70, RMSE 1.04) trained on 19,200 simulation trials predicts user acceptance and confidence for each intervention arm.
The bandit successfully learns to choose 'no intervention' for naive users (~46.7%), avoiding unnecessary calibration that would waste cognitive effort.
Simulation results show adaptive policies can match or exceed static calibration baselines while reducing unnecessary interventions.

faq.q: Why LinUCB instead of Thompson Sampling?
faq.a: LinUCB provides deterministic recommendations suitable for the experimental context where reproducibility across sessions is desirable. Thompson Sampling would be a natural alternative for future work, particularly if we extend to non-stationary reward distributions.

faq.q: Why does the naive user profile still show -0.4% below random?
faq.a: This is a ceiling effect: naive users already accept ~89% of outputs, so the bandit's exploration cost during learning outweighs potential improvement. The bandit correctly learns to choose 'no intervention' (46.7% of trials), which is the desired behavior — it just takes time to learn.

changelog.event: Simulation pipeline complete. XGBoost trained on 19,200 trials (AUC 0.70, RMSE 1.04)
Prototype: rule-based fallback + synthetic data generation
